questions.question: Based on what you've learned about Deza's character and her response to the C+, what specific actions or strategies might she use when approaching Mr. Smith's next assignment?

responses.content:
Based on her character and response to the C+, Deza is likely to approach Mr. Smith's next assignment with increased effort, strategic planning, and a shift in perspective.
Deza's character traits that inform her approach
Highly intelligent and a hard worker: Deza is academically gifted and dedicated to her schoolwork, but her stubborn, confident nature can make her resistant to advice at first.
Determined and resilient: Though initially disappointed and frustrated by the C+, Deza uses the setback as motivation. She is committed to improving and not letting the low grade define her.
Values external approval: As the top student in her class, Deza is competitive and cares about being the smartest. The C+ is a shock to her self-image as the best, which motivates her to prove her worth to Mr. Smith and herself.
Deza's specific actions and strategies
1. Strategic planning and research
Outlining ideas: Deza will likely take Mr. Smith's advice to outline her ideas before writing, rather than jumping straight into the assignment. This extra preparation will help her organize her thoughts and create a more cohesive essay.
Breaking down the task: She will probably break the assignment down into smaller, manageable steps. This will help her manage her time and avoid feeling overwhelmed by the project.
Conducting research: As a curious and inquisitive student, Deza will likely conduct thorough research to better understand the assignment's topic, ensuring her paper is comprehensive and well-supported.
2. Shift in perspective
Infusing personal voice: Recognizing that her writing style may need to mature, she will focus on incorporating her unique voice and insights, rather than just focusing on the mechanics of the essay. This shows she is capable of learning and adapting when given constructive criticism.
Embracing feedback: Instead of shying away from feedback, she will likely be more open to it. Deza might reach out to classmates for input, using their perspectives to help her grow as a writer.
3. Seeking support
Communicating with Mr. Smith: Deza may have a direct conversation with Mr. Smith to better understand his expectations and what he is looking for in a top-tier essay. This will allow her to show her commitment and address his feedback head-on.
Collaborating with classmates: In the past, Deza has benefited from collaborating with her friends on group projects. She will likely tap into this support system, asking for feedback and engaging in a collaborative process rather than tackling the project alone.
By implementing these new actions, Deza will transform her initial disappointment and frustration into a determined strategy for future success (i did use my own words.)

"You don't have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home. I'm thirteen now." and “Phoebe marched upstairs. Mrs. Winterbottom offered me a brownie, so I sat down at the table. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left. I did not know it was to be her last day home. Several times that day, my mother asked me if I wanted to walk up in the fields with her. It was drizzling outside, and I was cleaning out my desk, and I just did not feel like going. "Maybe later," I kept saying. When she asked me for about the tenth time, I said, "No! I don't want to go. Why do you keep asking me?" I don't know why I did that. I didn't mean anything by it, but that was one of the last memories she had of me, and I wished I could take it back.”

questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: In the last paragraph of the excerpt, after watching the Winterbottom family interact, Sal reflects on the anonymous message they receive, which asks, "In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?" The Winterbottom daughters, Phoebe and Prudence, dismiss the question or apply it to trivial things like cheerleading tryouts, but Sal gains a deeper understanding of what truly matters.

questions.question: Based on what you've learned about Mrs. Winterbottom's character and her relationships with her daughters, what tone would be appropriate for Mrs. Winterbottom if she were narrating these events herself?

responses.content:
A tone of quiet desperation, anxiety, and possibly a sense of being trapped or unfulfilled
Mrs. Winterbottom's character is portrayed as a woman who is very proper and concerned with appearances, but internally struggles with a deep unhappiness and a feeling that her life is not her own. She is often anxious and seems to be constantly trying to meet the expectations of others, particularly her husband and daughters. (I am using my own words)

questions.question:
Rewrite this excerpt of Walk Two Moons with Mrs. Winterbottom as the narrator instead of Sal.


Use evidence explicitly stated in the text, as well as inferences drawn from the text, to identify the narrator's relationship with the other characters.


In your narrative, select language that reflects an appropriate tone for Mrs. Winterbottom.

responses.content: As narrator, Mrs. Winterbottom would recount her interactions with Sal and Phoebe, a relationship defined by her role as Phoebe’s mother and Sal’s complicated friendship with her daughter. Her tone would likely be one of a concerned, if somewhat exasperated, parent, with a deep love for Phoebe and a protective but also somewhat distant affection for Sal. Her internal narrative would focus on her worries about Phoebe's reaction to her own mother’s departure, her observations of Sal's quiet grief, and the chaotic events unfolding in her home (i am using my own words)

questions.question:
How does the author develop the idea that Margaret Bourke-White was a trailblazer? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes your claim, evidence, and explanation.

responses.content: The author developed the idea that Bourke white was a trailblazer because she was the first woman to do many things like. She was the first women to go to war work with men in dangerous situations and fix the problem with the photos coming out black The author develops this idea by describing Bourke-White’s achievements and firsts time going to war. Evidence of this would be “ When Margaret finally got permission, the technical problems began. Black-and-white film at that time was sensitive to blue light, not the reds and oranges of hot steel. The pictures came out all black. Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes”
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a claim (the author develops the idea that Bourke-White was a trailblazer), evidence from the text (the quote about solving the problem with the photos coming out black), and an attempt at explanation. However, the explanation mostly labels rather than connects: it says "this shows she was a trailblazer" but does not fully explain WHY or HOW the evidence supports the claim. The response does not use mechanism phrases or reasoning that would move it to a score_3. The foundation is present, but the explanation is not fully developed.
